steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence (Weaken) question. The hypothesis is that CuO-NP bioaccumulation in L. stagnalis could serve as a reliable proxy for X. laevis bioaccumulation, eliminating the need for costly vertebrate sampling. To weaken this, we need a finding that undermines the assumption that L. stagnalis bioaccumulation reliably mirrors X. laevis bioaccumulation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'When L. stagnalis and X. laevis are exposed to similar CuO-NP levels, concentrations in both species show little variation from individual to individual': This actually strengthens the hypothesis by showing consistent, predictable bioaccumulation in both species. ✗
  • Choice B — 'The rate of CuO-NP uptake in L. stagnalis differs from the rate of CuO-NP uptake in X. laevis in a way that is not yet well understood by researchers': If the two species absorb nanoparticles at different and poorly understood rates, then L. stagnalis readings cannot reliably predict X. laevis readings — directly undermining the proxy hypothesis. ✓
  • Choice C — 'L. stagnalis accumulates several other types of manufactured nanoparticles, whereas X. laevis accumulates only CuO-NPs': This concerns other nanoparticles, not CuO-NP bioaccumulation comparability. It is irrelevant to the specific proxy hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Compared with X. laevis, L. stagnalis can accumulate detectable CuO-NP concentrations with significantly fewer negative effects': This is a practical advantage of using L. stagnalis, which supports rather than weakens the hypothesis. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
